clusters.title: Emergency relief announced as cleanup begins for ex-tropical Cyclone Narelle
clusters.neutral_headline: Ex Tropical Cyclone Narelle Hits Western Australia

items.title: Emergency relief announced as cleanup begins for ex-tropical Cyclone Narelle
items.standfirst: Structures have been destroyed and more than 1000 people remain without power as the cleanup begins in Western Australia following ex-tropical cyclone Narelle.
items.content: Structures have been destroyed and more than 1000 people remain without power as the cleanup begins in Western Australia following ex-tropical cyclone Narelle.

items.title: Airport 'obliterated', town cut off as powerful ex-cyclone lashes WA
items.standfirst: The tourist town of Exmouth in Western Australia was left completely cut off as the clean-up from ex-tropical cyclone Narelle begins .
items.content: Residents across Western Australia face a mammoth clean-up after ex-tropical cyclone Narelle left a trail of destruction in its wake. The tourist town of Exmouth, located about 1200 kilometres from Perth, bore the brunt of the wild storm, with the area completely cut off from water supplies and power.Narelle, a category-four system at its peak, blew wind gusts of up to 250km/h and dumped 350mm of rain on Exmouth as it tore through the town.READ MORE: Free public transport offered in one state as fuel crisis deepens READ MORE: The graph the government doesn't want you to see about our oil supplyThe single sealed road in and out of the Pilbara town was also cut off by floodwaters, isolating residents from the rest of the state.Work is now being done to restore the town's power and water. Exmouth's airport was "obliterated" during the storm event and residents face the daunting task of assessing the damage after Narelle weakened to a subtropical low.Shire president Matthew Nikkula said the town "looked like a war zone"."There is no current plan to get in and out," Nikkula also told 9News yesterday."Unfortunately air travel is ceased because of the damage to the runways and the terminals and we have no way of landing a plane or unloading or reloading it."Homeowner Brock Keymor told 9News he stepped outside to see part of his roof had been ripped off."Just a really loud banging noise - I thought it was a hole in the front of the building, where a bit of metal had come off," Keymor said."I didn't realise it was half the roof coming off." "It's trashed, the joint is trashed," resident Allan Allder told 9News.In preparing for the weather event, locals in Exmouth hit the shops to stockpile on supplies, which are now in short supply.Narelle is now tracking away from WA towards the south coast and into the Southern Ocean, where it is losing steam.READ MORE: The rise of Looksmaxxing: 'Creating shame and secrecy among young men' Crops devastated by Narelle Further south, Carnarvon banana growers were spared the direct hit first forecast but were still left devastated, after the powerful storm destroyed hundreds of thousands of dollars worth of crops.Banana grower Les Ball said he has lost upwards of 80 per cent of his crop in the regions third natural disaster so far this year. "It's not nice that's for sure, but its just the territory and the industry that we're in," Ball told 9News.It marks the third natural disaster this year for growers, who have already contended with a heatwave, as well as Cyclone Mitchell, which decimated crops last month.Despite the devastation, growers say it won't impact banana prices at the supermarket.Only about 15 percent of our bananas come from WA, the majority are from Queensland."It will be a while until you see a WA banana back on shelves but that's OK there's plenty in Queensland," Ball said.Narelle was downgraded to a category 2 system early this morning, after making landfall through Carnarvon's south-east.It is the second longest-living system behind Cyclone Nathan, according to the Bureau of Meteorology.Perth's wettest March day in 92 yearsResidents in Mandurah copped some of the worst rainfall totals, with more than 100mm coming down in 24 hours."[This] is well above the March average of only 15mm and a new record for the station," Jessica Lingard from the BoM said.It was also wet on the coast, with Swanbourne getting 78mm, while the city lashed with close to 63mm, making it Perth's wettest March day since 1934.The SES received more than 120 calls for help across the metro area since midnight."Quite a low number really, given the weather that we've had," Department of Fire and Emergency Services Commissioner Darren Klemm said.'By far the worst, the most scary'The cyclone made landfall yesterday for the third time, crossing south of Coral Bay around 3.15pm (6.15pm AEDT) as a category 3 storm, before whipping up winds of up to 250km/h at Exmouth.The holiday hotspot was drenched in a year's worth of rain in just 12 hours. The historic system - which had already hit Queensland and the Northern Territory before sweeping out to sea and re-intensifying - was expected to impact an 800km stretch of coastline from the Pilbara down through the Gascoyne region yesterday.READ MORE: Queensland could fast-track the opening of an oil lake the size of Singapore to ease pain of fuel crisis The crossing of the "very destructive core" came after the severe tropical cyclone pummelled parts of the West Australian coastline, ripping roofs from homes in Exmouth as it tracked south.One resident , who has lived through a number of cyclones in Exmouth, told 9News this was "by far the worst, the most scary" storm she's experienced.The weather system whipped up dust storms which painted Carnarvon and Shark Bay hues from bright orange to red."You could taste the dust, it was in your mouth, in your eyes and everything," Kerrie Shepherd from Shark Bay Caravan Park told 9News. "It was apocalyptic, it was bizarre, it was something I don't think many people will see it in their lives either.West Australian Premier Roger Cook said it was a confronting situation for residents and emergency services. "We know everyone in the north-west is made of tough stuff, but our thoughts are with you all," Cook said.Dozens of emergency services personnel were sent to the region before the storm hit to be in place for the clean up.READ MORE: Easter road trips canned, cafes weigh surcharge as fuel prices stingWhile holiday operators closed on Friday and and cancelled bookings just a week out from the Easter holidays."That's just the ride that we're on, and the place that we live in up here," Dave Gillespie from Exmouth Escape Resort told 9News."It's one of those extra burdens that we've got to put up with."NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

The tourist town of Exmouth was left completely cut off
Structures have been destroyed in the affected areas
Emergency relief has been announced for the affected regions
